The psalmist begins by looking up to the hills and asking a haunting question: where does help come from? There is something profoundly honest in this moment. The hills represent something larger than oneself—strength, permanence, security. But the psalmist does not trust them. Instead, he pivots immediately to answer his own question with absolute clarity: help comes from the LORD, the Maker of heaven and earth. This is not a wavering hope or a desperate guess. The God who spoke everything into existence, who holds the universe in His hands, is the source of real help. Once you have located your help in the right place, everything changes. You are no longer looking to the world's strength or your own resources. You are looking to the God who transcends all of it.

What follows is a breathtaking portrait of a God who never rests. While you sleep, while you walk through danger, while you work and stumble through your days, the LORD neither slumbers nor sleeps. This is not poetic exaggeration meant to comfort the anxious—it is a statement about the nature of God Himself. He does not grow weary. He does not miss the moment when you falter. He does not take a break from watching over you. The promise is so comprehensive that it covers every direction: He is your shade on your right hand, protecting you from the harshness of sun and moon alike. No danger—whether visible or hidden, whether comes by day or by night—escapes His notice.

But there is something demanding in this psalm that we must not soften. The protection offered here is not freedom from difficulty or pain. Verse 7 promises that "the LORD will guard you from all evil," yet the world remains full of suffering. What the psalm actually guarantees is that no evil will ultimately separate you from God or thwart His purposes for your soul. Your keeper watches over you not to make your life comfortable, but to preserve your soul—to keep you, steadfast and secure, in His hands. This is a psalm for those in genuine danger, for those who know their own weakness, for those who cannot protect themselves. It calls you to stop looking at the hills and start looking to the God who made them. When you truly believe that the Maker of heaven and earth is your keeper, your coming and your going, both now and forevermore, you are freed from the tyranny of fear. Not because nothing bad will happen, but because nothing can happen that will separate you from Him.

This is the God you worship—watchful, tireless, sovereign over all that threatens you. The question is not whether you will face difficulty. The question is whether you will answer as the psalmist does: my help comes from the LORD.
